title: Go vs. Python: What a Weather App Taught Me About Concurrency (Part I)
author: Dustin
text:
I Built the Same Weather App in Go and Python — Here’s What It Taught Me About Parallel Programming
Why different languages can solve the same problem — and still think very differently
Most programming concepts feel abstract until you build something real with them.
This project started that way.
On paper, it was “just” a semester assignment where I had to compare parallel programming concepts across languages. In practice, it turned into something much more interesting: a small but realistic system that forced me to confront how Go and Python actually think about concurrency.
So I did what I usually do when I want to understand something properly:
I built the same program twice.
Same requirements.
Same problem.
Different philosophies.
The Problem I Chose (and Why It’s Not Trivial)
You probably know this situation:
You check the weather for tomorrow.
You open three different weather apps.
You get three different answers.
And instinctively, you trust… the one you like most 🙂
But that made me wonder:
What if we didn’t choose
what if we aggregated
That question became the core of this project.
I built a CLI weather aggregator that:
- takes a city name
- fetches weather data from multiple APIs at the same time
- handles failures gracefully
- aggregates temperature, humidity, and conditions
- shows both individual results and a consensus
And then I implemented it twice:
- once in Go
- once in Python
Why Go and Python?
I didn’t pick these languages randomly.
- Python, because I love it. It’s expressive, fast to write, and fantastic for prototyping.
- Go, because concurrency is not an add-on — it’s the point.
They sit at almost opposite ends of the language-design spectrum, which makes the comparison meaningful.
Same task.
Different mental models.
Concurrency vs. Parallelism (Before We Touch Code)
Before diving in, one clarification matters:
- Concurrency means structuring work so tasks overlap.
- Parallelism means executing work at the same time on multiple cores.
This project is I/O-bound (HTTP requests).
So the real win comes from overlapping waiting times, not from raw CPU power.
That’s important — because both languages handle this well, but very differently.
What the Program Does (High-Level)
The application:
- accepts a city name (Unicode-safe: München, São Paulo, etc.)
- geocodes it once
- queries up to five weather APIs
- applies timeouts and error handling
- aggregates results even if some APIs fail
Weather data from three sources is still better than none.
That decision — graceful degradation — turned out to matter more than the language choice.
The First Big Insight: Philosophy Shows Up Everywhere
Before touching performance numbers or syntax, something stood out immediately:
Go
Concurrency is the default mental model.
Get Dustin’s stories in your inbox
Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
You don’t ask:
“Should I do this concurrently?”
You ask:
“Why wouldn’t I?”
Goroutines and channels are built into the language. You don’t opt into them — you grow into them naturally.
Python
Concurrency is explicit and optional.
You can:
- write synchronous code
- add asyncio
- use threads
- use multiprocessing
That flexibility is powerful — but it also means you are responsible for choosing correctly.
The Weather Example Makes This Concrete
Fetching weather data sounds simple.
But real-world details creep in fast:
- different APIs expect different inputs
- some want city names, others want coordinates
- free tiers are unreliable
- rate limits exist
- response formats don’t match
- weather codes mean different things everywhere
Suddenly, concurrency isn’t just a performance trick — it’s a design requirement.
A First Look at the Code (Simplified)
Go: Concurrency Feels Natural
In Go, fetching from multiple APIs concurrently looks almost boringly straightforward:
results := make(chan WeatherResult)
for _, api := range apis {
go func(api WeatherAPI) {
result, err := api.Fetch(city)
results <- WeatherResult{Data: result, Err: err}
}(api)
}
Then you collect results:
for i := 0; i < len(apis); i++ {
res := <-results
if res.Err == nil {
aggregated = append(aggregated, res.Data)
}
}
No event loop.
No explicit scheduler.
Concurrency is just… there.
More in the Part II.
Why This Is a Good Comparison Problem
This project worked well as a comparison because:
- It’s I/O-bound
- It requires timeouts
- It must survive partial failures
- It needs coordination, not just speed
In other words: the exact kind of problem where concurrency models reveal their strengths and weaknesses.
Where This Is Going (Part II)
In the next part, I’ll go deeper into:
- how Go’s goroutines + channels actually coordinate work (you will see my coding)
- how Python’s asyncio event loop approaches the same problem
- why both produce surprisingly similar code structures
- and where the real differences show up — not in syntax, but in mental load
If you’ve ever wondered why Go concurrency feels “easy”
— or why Python async sometimes feels fragile —
that’s where things get interesting.
If you want to look at the full implementation, I’ll happily share the GitHub repository — just ask in the comments.
I’m Dustin — an applied AI and coding enthusiast exploring how math, code, and intuition connect.
This post is part of my “Go” mini-series, where I make programming concepts feel a little more human.
If you found this article informative and valuable, I’d greatly appreciate your support:
Give it a few claps 👏 on Medium to help others discover this content (did you know you can clap up to 50 times?). Your claps will help spread the knowledge to more readers.
See you!
— Dustin
